deck.name: Nature & Environment
deck.description: Vocabulary for ecology, climate change, wildlife, geography, and sustainability.

cards.definition: the ability to be maintained at a steady level without exhausting natural resources
cards.context: Environmental sustainability is one of the biggest challenges of our time.

cards.definition: the total amount of greenhouse gases produced by an individual or organization
cards.context: Switching to solar power can significantly reduce your carbon footprint.

cards.definition: the variety of life in the world or in a particular habitat
cards.context: The rainforest is home to an incredible level of biodiversity.

cards.definition: energy from a source that is not depleted when used (e.g., wind, solar)
cards.context: Many countries are investing heavily in renewable energy sources.

cards.definition: the natural home or environment of an animal, plant, or other organism
cards.context: Deforestation is destroying the natural habitat of many endangered species.

cards.definition: a biological community of interacting organisms and their physical environment
cards.context: Even small changes can disrupt the delicate balance of an ecosystem.

cards.definition: a gradual increase in the overall temperature of the earth's atmosphere
cards.context: Global warming is causing glaciers to melt at an alarming rate.

cards.definition: the trapping of the sun's warmth in the planet's lower atmosphere
cards.context: Certain gases contribute to the greenhouse effect by trapping heat.

cards.definition: the protection of plants, animals, and natural resources
cards.context: Wildlife conservation efforts have helped save several species from extinction.

cards.definition: the action of clearing a wide area of trees
cards.context: Deforestation is a major contributor to climate change.

cards.definition: the presence in or introduction into the environment of a substance which has harmful effects
cards.context: Air pollution is a serious health concern in many large cities.

cards.definition: the production and discharge of something, especially gas or radiation
cards.context: The government is trying to cut down on vehicle emissions.

cards.definition: natural fuels such as coal or gas, formed from the remains of living organisms
cards.context: We need to move away from fossil fuels to cleaner energy.

cards.definition: a species of animal or plant that is at risk of extinction
cards.context: The giant panda is one of the world's most famous endangered species.

cards.definition: a lifestyle or philosophy of minimizing trash and recycling everything
cards.context: She is trying to live a zero waste lifestyle by using reusable containers.

cards.definition: plastic items used once and then thrown away
cards.context: Many places have banned single-use plastic straws and bags.

cards.definition: produced without the use of chemical fertilizers, pesticides, or other artificial agents
cards.context: I prefer to buy organic vegetables whenever possible.

cards.definition: all the visible features of an area of countryside or land
cards.context: The mountain landscape was absolutely breathtaking.

cards.definition: the natural features of a landscape considered in terms of their appearance
cards.context: We stopped several times to admire the beautiful scenery.

cards.definition: the plants and animals of a particular region, habitat, or geological period
cards.context: The island has a unique variety of flora and fauna found nowhere else.

cards.definition: the plants and animals that live in the ocean
cards.context: Climate change is having a devastating impact on marine life.

cards.definition: a stretch of land, especially with regard to its physical features
cards.context: The rugged terrain made the hike quite difficult.

cards.definition: the regular rise and fall of the level of the sea
cards.context: Be careful when walking on the beach; the tide is coming in.

cards.definition: a slowly moving mass or river of ice
cards.context: Tourists come from all over the world to see the massive glaciers.

cards.definition: a prolonged period of abnormally low rainfall, leading to a shortage of water
cards.context: The region is suffering from its worst drought in fifty years.

cards.definition: a layer in the earth's stratosphere that absorbs most of the sun's ultraviolet radiation
cards.context: The hole in the ozone layer is slowly beginning to heal.

cards.definition: the process of replanting an area with trees
cards.context: Reforestation projects are essential for restoring lost forests.

cards.definition: decayed organic material used as a plant fertilizer
cards.context: We turn our food scraps into compost for the garden.

cards.definition: making no net release of carbon dioxide to the atmosphere
cards.context: The company aims to be carbon neutral by 2030.

cards.definition: the benefits people obtain from ecosystems (e.g., clean water, pollination)
cards.context: Bees provide essential ecosystem services through pollination.

cards.definition: an economic system aimed at eliminating waste and the continual use of resources
cards.context: The transition to a circular economy is vital for long-term sustainability.

cards.definition: the point at which a series of small changes or incidents becomes significant enough to cause a larger, important change
cards.context: Scientists worry we are approaching a climate tipping point.

cards.definition: the establishment of a forest or stand of trees in an area where there was no previous tree cover
cards.context: Large-scale afforestation projects can help absorb carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.

cards.definition: extremely small pieces of plastic debris in the environment resulting from the disposal and breakdown of consumer products and industrial waste
cards.context: Microplastics have been found in the deepest parts of the ocean.

cards.definition: a thick subsurface layer of soil that remains frozen throughout the year
cards.context: Thawing permafrost releases large amounts of methane, a potent greenhouse gas.

cards.definition: a strip of natural habitat connecting populations of wildlife otherwise separated by cultivated land, roads, or other human activities
cards.context: Wildlife corridors allow animals to move safely between protected areas.

cards.definition: the process of restoring an area of land to its natural uncultivated state
cards.context: Rewilding projects aim to reintroduce native species to their former habitats.

cards.definition: the reduction in the pH of the ocean over an extended period of time, caused primarily by uptake of carbon dioxide
cards.context: Ocean acidification is a major threat to coral reefs.

cards.definition: fog or haze combined with smoke and other atmospheric pollutants
cards.context: Many industrial cities suffer from heavy smog during the winter months.

cards.definition: the process of recycling organic matter, such as food scraps and leaves, into a valuable fertilizer
cards.context: Composting at home is an easy way to reduce the amount of waste sent to landfills.

cards.definition: violence or destruction carried out in the name of environmentalism (controversial term)
cards.context: The group was accused of ecoterrorism after damaging the logging equipment.

cards.definition: the practice of making an unsubstantiated or misleading claim about the environmental benefits of a product, service, technology or company practice
cards.context: The company was criticized for greenwashing its image without making real changes.

cards.definition: a person who advocates or acts for the protection and preservation of the environment and wildlife
cards.context: The conservationist worked tirelessly to protect the local wetlands.

cards.definition: catching too many fish at once, so the breeding population becomes too depleted to recover
cards.context: Overfishing has led to a collapse in several major fish stocks.

cards.definition: a substance used for destroying insects or other organisms harmful to cultivated plants or to animals
cards.context: The use of chemical pesticides can have harmful effects on non-target species like bees.
